question.text: Scenario: Mr. Suresh Kumar, aged 40, works in the automobile industry and recently faced a salary reduction from Rs. 1,00,000/month to Rs. 75,000/month due to an industry slowdown. He has the following loans: (1) Home loan: Rs. 28 lakh outstanding at 9% p.a. fixed rate, EMI Rs. 28,000, remaining tenure 12 years; (2) Car loan: Rs. 3 lakh outstanding at 12% p.a., EMI Rs. 8,500, remaining tenure 3.5 years; (3) Education loan for son: Rs. 5 lakh at 10% p.a., EMI Rs. 10,600, remaining tenure 5 years. His non-debt monthly expenses are Rs. 35,000. He has savings of Rs. 4 lakh in FDs and Rs. 1.5 lakh in savings bank. His wife earns Rs. 25,000/month from tutoring. The bank has offered to restructure his home loan by extending tenure from 12 to 18 years at the same 9% rate, which would reduce the home loan EMI to approximately Rs. 22,000. Question 4 of 5: Suresh's current liquidity ratio (liquid assets = FDs Rs. 4 lakh + Savings bank Rs. 1.5 lakh, monthly expenses = Rs. 82,100 before restructuring) is:

explanation.text: **The Logic:** Liquid assets = Rs. 4,00,000 + Rs. 1,50,000 = Rs. 5,50,000. Monthly expenses (total including debt servicing) = Rs. 82,100 (before restructuring). Liquidity ratio = 5,50,000 / 82,100 = 6.70. **The Trap:** Using only non-debt expenses (Rs. 35,000) in the denominator gives 5,50,000/35,000 = 15.7 which overstates the ratio. Include all monthly obligations. **Cross-Exam Context:** Liquidity ratio during financial stress is tested in X-A (Ch 3). **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** A ratio of 6.7 months is actually comfortable — Suresh should NOT touch these liquid assets for debt repayment. They're his safety net during the income reduction period.
